subject: Oncology
title: Breast cancer
preambleMarkdown:
# Breast cancer



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Breast cancer is a neoplastic, premalignant, or tumor-related topic in oncology.
- This article focuses on tumor biology, staging, histology, treatment intent, oncologic emergencies, survivorship, and palliation.
- The practical clinical priority is malignant potential, staging, treatment intent, and complications.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Breast cancer to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Breast cancer is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is malignant potential, staging, treatment intent, and complications.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Benign, premalignant, in situ, localized invasive, regional, metastatic, recurrent, or treatment-related state.
- Histology, grade, stage, biomarkers, and performance status.
- Curative, disease-control, surveillance, symptom-relief, or end-of-life treatment intent.
- Screen-detected, symptomatic, incidental, familial, or therapy-associated presentation.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Breast cancer depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In oncology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Breast cancer.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Breast cancer disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Breast cancer should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Breast cancer first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Breast cancer, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Mass effect, bleeding, obstruction, pain, weight loss, constitutional symptoms, paraneoplastic findings, or incidental screening abnormality may occur.
- Red flags depend on anatomic site and include neurologic compromise, airway compromise, uncontrolled bleeding, or sepsis.
- Treatment history matters because recurrence and treatment toxicity can mimic new disease.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Breast cancer, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused oncology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Breast cancer presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Breast cancer with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Breast cancer is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Breast cancer should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis with tissue when required, stage before definitive therapy when possible, and define treatment intent.
- Use multidisciplinary planning for surgery, radiation, systemic therapy, surveillance, fertility, rehabilitation, and palliation.
- Treat oncologic emergencies immediately while definitive cancer planning continues.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Breast cancer is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses malignant potential, staging, treatment intent, and complications.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical referral is needed for resectable disease, biopsy planning, obstruction, bleeding, perforation, cord compression, airway compromise, or palliation.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Breast cancer, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Breast cancer may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Breast cancer patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Breast cancer, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Breast cancer in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Breast cancer, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Breast cancer does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Breast cancer may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Breast cancer, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Breast cancer as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis and staging are separate steps.
- Treatment depends on stage, histology, biomarkers, performance status, comorbidity, and patient goals.
- Know emergency presentations: cord compression, tumor lysis, febrile neutropenia, airway compromise, and superior vena cava syndrome.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Breast cancer draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Breast Cancer

_Source lines: 31382-31459._

```text
Breast Cancer
A. Risk factors include prior history of breast cancer, age, family history
of breast cancer, female gender, prior thoracic radiation, smoking,
alcohol consumption, and anything which increases the number of
menstrual cycles (early menarche, late menopause, nulliparity).

B. Breast cancer is rare in women under 35 years-think of fibroadenoma
if a young woman presents with a round, movable mass which
changes in size over the course of the menstrual cycle.

C. The differential diagnosis for a breast mass at any age includes cysts

(benign if not bloody after aspiration and does not recur) and
fibrocystic changes (bilateral breast pain caused by cyclic hormonal
stimulation).

D. The premalignant in situ breast cancers are ductal carcinoma in situ
(DCIS) and lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS)-distinguished
histologically:
1. DCIS represents a broad spectrum of disease which arises from the
ductal elements of the breast. It is often found on screening
mammogram, and a mass may or may not be present. As this is a
marker for the possible development of invasive ductal carcinoma
(typically in the same breast), treat with lumpectomy (or
mastectomy if negative margins cannot be achieved). Radiation and
systemic chemotherapy are used less often depending on the
specific lesion in question.

2. LCIS arises from the lobular elements of the breast. The lesion is
typically found incidentally during biopsy of another breast lesion,
and a mass is rarely palpable. It leads to an increased risk of breast
cancer in either breast. Unfortunately, removal of the lesion does
not reduce the risk of progression to invasive cancer. Treatment is
variable, but may include close observation, selective estrogen
receptor modulators, and prophylactic bilateral mastectomy.

E. The two most common types of breast cancer are infiltrating ductal
carcinoma, accounting for over 70% of invasive breast cancers, and
infiltrating lobular carcinoma.

1. Patients may present with a palpable mass, palpable lymph nodes,
skin dimpling, nipple retraction, or no symptoms at all (found on
mammogram).

2. Diagnosis should be confirmed with tissue biopsy, which should
include testing for the estrogen/progesterone receptor and HER-2/
neu amplification status.

3. Treatment typically begins with surgery with adjuvant radiotherapy
(if the tumor has not spread to sentinel lymph nodes) to decrease
tumor recurrence.

4. Adjuvant chemotherapy is required for large lesions in
premenopausal women, but is typically not necessary for all small
lesions (typically <1 cm) without lymph node involvement.

5. Postmenopausal women with large lesions or lymph node
involvement may receive either chemotherapy or an aromatase
inhibitor (anastrozole, letrozole), depending on the status of the
estrogen receptor. Tamoxifen may be used as an alternate agent for
those who cannot tolerate an aromatase inhibitor.

Plasma Cell Disorders
Monoclonal Gammopathy of Undetermined
Significance
- Common in the elderly (over 5% in patients >70 years of age)
- Asymptomatic, premalignant clonal plasma cell proliferation
- Diagnosis is confirmed by the presence of monoclonal protein (M-
protein) spike <3.0 g/dL and <10% plasma cells in bone marrow
without evidence of multiple myeloma (i.e., lytic bone lesions, anemia,
hypercalcemia, or renal insuficiency)
- About 1% of people with MGUS will develop multiple myeloma each
year. Patients can be risk stratified by multiple laboratory parameters
- No specific treatment is necessary (just close observation)
```

<!-- source: lib.md import end -->

### Source Excerpt: Breast Cancer

_Source lines: 13812-14312._

```text
II. Breast Cancer
A. Noninvasive breast cancer
l . D CIS
a. Pathology
( l ) Proliferation of malignant epithelial cells is confined by the basement
membrane of the duct-lobular system.

B R E A S T e 1 89

(2) Classification: comedo, cribriform, micropapillary, papillary, solid
(a) Up to 60% of mass may be mixed.

(b) Newer systems use grading of D CIS-high-, intermediate-, and
low-grade.

(3) Cannot spread to lymphatics because the disease is confined to the duct

## 2 . Clinical presentation

a. Nipple discharge , Paget disease of the nipple , mass
3. Mammographic presentation (most common)
a. Pleomorphic microcalcifications
b. Frequency of diagnosis of D CIS is increasing because of mammography.

( l ) DCIS represents 30% to 50% of malignancies seen on biopsy of suspicious
mammogram abnormalities.

(2) DCIS may also be an incidental finding when invasive cancer is present.

4. Surgical management
a. Lumpectomy and radiation: most common treatment (breast conservation
therapy)
( l ) NSABP-B l 7 (randomized trial studying lumpectomy with and without
radiation therapy)
(a) Overall survival rates of lumpectomy both with and without radiation
compared favorably with those of mastectomy.

(b) There was a clear reduction in local recurrence when lumpectomy was
combined with radiation, but this did not affect overall survival.

b. Mastectomy
( l ) Curative in about 98% of patients
(2) Up to 26% of patients may have invasive cancer not identified preopera
tively. Invasive cancer is usually found in larger, high-grade DCIS lesions.

(3) Mastectomy is usually reserved for diffuse DCIS throughout the breast or
because of patient preference.

(4) In performing mastectomy for DCIS, sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB)
can be used in the event that incidental invasive cancer is identified on
Hnal pathology.

c. Excision alone
( l ) Silverstein and associates developed the Van Nuys prognostic index
(Table l l-2) dependent on certain risk factors: size, surgical margins,
nuclear grade, and comedo necrosis.

(a) Patients with a score of 3 to 4 can be treated with lumpectomy alone.

(b) Scores of 5 to 7 require the addition of radiation therapy.

(2) Excision alone may be offered to some patients with small, favorable
tumors with wide, negative surgical margins.

d. Adjuvant therapy for DCIS
(l) Tamoxifen, as determined by NSABP-B24, was found to reduce the risk of
ipsilateral breast cancer recurrence by 38% and contralateral disease by 52%.

(a) It is recommended for patients with estrogen receptor (ER)-positive
tumors.

(2) Systemic chemotherapy is not needed for DCIS alone.

(3) Radiation therapy is recommended in most patients undergoing lumpec
tomy with the possible exception being some patients with small, favorable
tumors and widely negative margins.

B. Invasive cancer
l . Types
a. Ductal: the most common type (approximately 80%)
b. Lobular: second most common type
( l ) Can be difficult to detect on mammography
(2) Consider bilateral breast MRI.

c. Mammary: tumor with ductal and lobular features
d. Medullary: less likely to have axillary node involvement
e. Others (incidence in parenthesis)
( l ) Mucoid or colloid (2.4%)
(2) Tubular ( 1 . 2%)
(3) Adenoid cystic (0.4%)
(4) Cribriform (0.3%)
(5) Carcinosarcoma (0. 1 %)
( 6) Papillary

## 2. Pathology

a. InUltration of cells across the basement membrane and into surrounding
stroma
b. Final pathology should contain tumor types, histologic grade, and hormonal
receptor status.

c. ERs and progesterone receptors (PRs) are members of a nuclear hormone
receptor family that act as transcription factors when bound by their
ligands .

( l ) ER-positive tumors account for approximately 70% of breast cancers.

(2) The greatest numbers of receptor-positive tumors are seen in well-
differentiated tumors and in tumors in older postmenopausal women.

d. HER2/neu protein is a member of the tyrosine kinase receptor family.

HER2/neu gene is amplified in almost one-third of breast cancer
patients .

( l ) This is associated with a shortened disease-free survival and a decreased
overall survival.

## 3 . Diagnosis

a. History (see earlier)
b. Physical examination (see earlier)
c. Imaging
( l ) All patients need bilateral diagnostic mammogram.

(2) Ultrasound for all palpable masses
(3) Consider adding bilateral breast MRI in certain circumstances.

d. Biopsy
( l ) All patients with palpable lesions can undergo core or vacuum-assisted
needle biopsy without image guidance, but image guidance decreases false
negative rate.

(2) All patients with nonpalpable abnormalities need image-guided core
biopsy, which can be either ultrasound, stereotactic (mammo) , or MRI
guided.

(3) Use image-guided modality that shows lesion best.

e. Surgical management
( l ) Lumpectomy (also known as partial mastectomy, segmentectomy, or wide
local excision)
(a) Most commonly recommended for stages ll disease, depending on
patient preference
(b) Surgeon must achieve negative surgical margins.

(c) If the tissue removed does not have negative margins, additional
operations to remove more tissue may be necessary.

(d) Specimen should be oriented.

(e) May use neoadjuvant chemotherapy for larger tumors or low breast to
rumor ratio to shrink the tumor in order to perform lumpectomy
CD Contraindications to lumpectomy (Table l l-3)

ge- Scientific Principles and Practice. 4th ed. Philadelphia. PA: Lippincott W i l l i a m s & W i l kins; 2006.1
(2) Mastectomy (with or without immediate reconstruction)
(a) Borders: superiorly to inferior border of clavicle, medial to the lateral
steIal border, laterally to the latissimus dorsi muscle, and inferiorly
to the superior border of the rectus sheath
(b) Performed for patients with large tumors, all patients with inflamma
tory breast cancer, and for patient preference
(c) Skin-sparing mastectomy (removal of nipple-areolar complex only) is
used in patients who desire immediate reconstruction.

(3) Lumpectomy versus mastectomy (NSABP-B06)
(a) Enrolled 1 ,257 patients who either had total mastectomy, lumpectomy
alone, or lumpectomy with radiation therapy
(b) Incidence of local recurrence was approximately 10% for those with
mastectomy, 9% with lumpectomy alone, and 3% for those with
lumpectomy with radiation.

(c) There is no difference in disease-free survival.

f. Axillary staging: required for invasive cancer
( 1 ) SLNB
(a) Lymphatics of the breast drain into the axilla and intramammary
chain, and the first nodes in either lymphatic basin are the sentinel
lymph nodes (SLNs) .

(b) SLNs can be identiEed in two ways:
i. Radionucleotide (technetium-labeled sulfur colloid)
(i) Radioactivity is measured by a handheld gamma probe, and
nodes are removed surgically.

ii. Isosulfan blue dye (peritumoral or subareolar inj ection)
(i) Blue lymph nodes are removed surgically.

iii. SLNs are blue, radioactive, or both.

iv. Be wary of firm, enlarged lymph nodes in the axilla that are
neither blue nor radioactive because they may be completely
replaced by tumor and therefore unable to uptake tracers.

(c) SLNB can be performed with lumpectomy or mastectomy.

(d) If SLN is negative for tumor, no further axillary surgery is
necessary.

(e) If SLN is positive for tumor, the current recommendation is an axillary
lymph node dissection (ALND) .

CD SLNB is not recommended for patients with clinically or histologically
involved axillary nodes and in inflammatory cancer, and it is contrain
dicated in pregnancy.

(g) Ultrasound-guided biopsy of abnormal nodes can help direct surgical
intervention.

(2) ALND
(a) Definition: removal of level l and 2 axillary nodes
(b) Boundaries of ALND
i. Superior: axillary vein
ii. Posterior: latissimus dorsi
iii. Anterior: pectoralis major and pectoralis minor
iv. Medial: serratus anterior
(c) Morbidic associated with ALND includes lymphedema ( 10% to 15%) ,
decreased range of motion at the shoulder, and numbness of the axilla
anor arm.

4. Staging (Tables l l -4 and l l -5)
5. Systemic therapy (www.nccn. org)
a. Chemotherapy
( l ) Should be considered for all patients with receptor-negative disease
(2) Should be considered for all patients with positive nodal involvement
(3) Should be considered for all patients with tumors greater than l em

(Reproduced with permission from M u l h o l l a n d MW, L i l lemoe K D , Doherty G M , Maier RV, Upchurch G R , eds. Greenfield's
Surgery: Scientific Principles and Practice. 4th ed. Philadelphia, PA: Lippi ncott Wi l l iams & W i l kins; 2006.)

(4) Should be considered for HERAneu-positive patients in conjunction with
Herceptin
(5) Most common regimen includes Adriamycin and cyclophosphamide.

(a) Side effects of Adriamycin include cardiotoxicity, bone marrow
suppression, fatigue, and hair loss.

(6) A taxane may be added for tumors with poor prognosis or increased lymph
node involvement.

(a) Side effects of taxanes include neuropathy and arthralgias.

b. Antihormonal therapy: for ER-positive and PR-positive cancers
( l ) Selective estrogen receptor modulators (SERM) (tamoxifen)
(a) Only antihormonal agent for premenopausal women
(b) When used for 5 years, it is associated with a significant reduction in
the risk of recurrence and the risk of dying from breast cancer in the
affected and contralateral breast.

(c) Side effects include hot flashes, small increase risk of deep vein
thrombosis (including pulmonary embolus) , and small increase risk of
uterine cancer.

(d) Can be used in premenopausal and postmenopausal women
(2) Aromatase inhibitors (AI)
(a) Inhibit the synthesis of estrogens from androgens
(b) Only used in postmenopausal patients
(c) The Arimidex or Tamoxifen Alone or in Combination (ATAC) trial
showed advantages to Arimidex over tamoxifen for postmenopausal
women with early stage, hormone receptor-positive disease.

(d) Als have fewer rare but serious side effects in comparison to
tamoxifen, but the Als are associated with more bone loss and
fractures.

(e) ATAC trial showed that Arimidex is better than tamoxifen in:
i. Preventing or delaying recurrence
ii. Lowering the risk of contralateral breast cancer

c. Antibody therapy (trastuzumab)
( l ) Trastuzumab (Herceptin) is a murine monoclonal antibody that can be
used to treat HER2/neu-positive cancers.

(2) Clinical studies have confirmed that trastuzumab, in combination with
Adriamycin or taxanes, improves survival in patients with HER2/neu
overexpression in metastatic disease.

(3) Used only in patients with HER2/neu overexpression
6. Radiation therapy
a. High-energy beams of radiation are focused on the affected breast.

b . In whole-breast radiation, patients receive radiation treatment as an
outpatient in daily sessions over 5 to 7 weeks . Delivery is delayed until after
chemotherapy if chemotherapy is required.

c. In partial breast radiation, patients receive focused radiation to the
lumpectomy cavity and l em of surrounding tissue twice daily for 5 days .

( l ) This is a newer form of radiation delivery, and long-term trials are still
necessary for validation.

d. Radiation after mastectomy
( l ) Tumor is greater than 5 em.

(2) Positive margins of resection
(3) Four or more lymph nodes with metastatic disease
e. Radiation after lumpectomy
( l ) Broadly speaking, radiation is necessary aer lumpectomy to affect
adequate local control.

f. Radiation is felt to be a contraindication or a relative contraindication in the
following cases:
(l) Prior radiation to the affected area of the body
(2) Patient with connective tissue disorders like lupus or vasculitis
(3) Pregnancy
(4) Unable to commit to the rigorous schedule required to complete treatment
Ill. Reconstruction
A. Types
l . Implant-based reconstruction: simplest form of breast reconstruction. Uses a
saline or silicone gel implant to recreate breast mound. Carries higher risks in
radiated fields .

a. One-stage reconstruction: performed at the same time as the mastectomy.

A permanent implant is placed in the subpectoral plane with or without the
use of acellular dermal matrix (ADM) .

b. Two-stage reconstruction: The Hrst stage involves placement of a temporary
implant called a tissue expander. The tissue expander is placed in the
subpectoral plane with or without the use of ADM. The patient then
undergoes weekly tissue expansions with saline inj ections. Once the skin
envelope and muscle are fully expanded, the tissue expander is removed
and a permanent implant is placed.

c. ADM: a biologic product made Xom acellular cadaveric dermis . It is used as
a sling along the lower pole of the pectoralis major muscle to help support
the tissue expander/implant, improve lower pole proj ection, and control
position of the inframammary fold. Carries higher risk of seroma formation.

## 2 . Autologous tissue reconstruction: uses the patient's own tissue to recreate

breast mound. Most complex form of reconstruction. Offers the most natural
appearance and feel. Recommended in the setting of reconstruction following
radiation therapy.

a. Pedicled transverse rectus abdominis muscle (TRAM) flap : Skin, subcutaneous
tissue, and muscle from the lower abdomen are transferred to the chest to
recreate a breast mound. SacriHces rectus abdominis muscle. Carries higher
risk of abdominal bulge or hernia formation.

b. Free tissue transfers: Tissue is harvested from another area of the body with
a vascular pedicle attached. The tissue is then transplanted to the breast and
B R E A S T e 1 95

Prophylactic m a stectomy
reduces the risk of b reast
c a n c e r by only a p p roximately
9 0 % ff n ot 1 00 % ) .

a microvascular anastomosis i s performed under a microscope . Common
donor sites include abdominal tissue (deep inferior epigastric perforator
[ DIEP ] flap, superficial inferior epigastric artery [ SIEA] flap) , buttock
tissue (superior gluteal artery perforator [ SGAP ] flap, inferior gluteal artery
perforator [ IGAP ] flap) , and thigh tissue (transverse upper gracilis [TUG]
flap, anterolateral thigh [ALT ] flap) . Usually does not involve muscle
sacrifice ; therefore, there is less donor site morbidity.

c. Latissimus dorsi flap: Muscle with or without a skin paddle is transferred
from the back to the breast. The latissimus is a broad, flat muscle and does
not offer significant volume or proj ection. Therefore, an implant is typically
placed beneath the muscle.

3. Nipple areolar reconstruction: Using either the native breast skin or flap skin
paddle, a nipple is created from a multilobed skin flap . There are numerous
flap designs , including skate flap , star flap , and so forth. The areola can be
created using a skin graft. Tattooing is used to give pigment and create color.

3-D tattoos are now being used to recreate the whole nascent polypeptide
associated complex (NAC) without the need for additional surgery.

B. Timing
l . Immediate reconstruction: performed in the same setting as the mastectomy.

Offers the best esthetic results .

2. Delayed reconstruction: performed months to years following the mastectomy.

Preferable in more advanced-stage cancers or inflammatory breast cancer.

More challenging to reconstruct due to loss of skin envelope .

## 3 . Delayed-immediate reconstruction: attempts t o convert a patient who is

known to require postmastectomy radiation from a traditional delayed
reconstruction into an immediate-type reconstruction. The patient undergoes
a skin-sparing mastectomy, and a tissue expander is placed. The expander is
fully inflated. The patient then undergoes radiation therapy with the expander
in place . The patient then undergoes definitive reconstruction with autologous
tissue 6 to 12 months following completion of radiation.
```

### Source Excerpt: Patients at High Risk for Breast Cancer

_Source lines: 14313-14397._

```text
PATIENTS AT HIG H RISK FOR BREAST CANCER		a	a	a	a	a	a
A. Definition
l . Hereditary: BRCA-positive status or with a strong family history of breast or
ovarian cancer
2. Proliferative disorders : personal history of ADH, atypical lobular hyperplasia
(ALH ) , or LCIS .

B. Increased monitoring
l . Augment screening mammogram with liberal use of ultrasound.

2. Addition of screening bilateral breast MRI alternating every 6 months with
screening mammography in BRCA-positive patients
C. Strategies to reduce the risk of breast cancer
l . Tamoxifen, a SERM
a. National Surgical Adjuvant Breast and Bowel Project (NSABP)-P l showed that
women taking tamoxifen had a 49% reduction in risk of invasive breast cancer
and a 50% reduction in risk of noninvasive breast cancer in ER-positive tumors.

b. Tamoxifen reduced the risk of breast cancer in women with personal history of
LCIS and AH by 65% and 85%, respectively.

2. Raloxifene, a newer SERM , may reduce the risk of breast cancer in ER-positive
postmenopausal patients .

a. NSABP P-2 (Study of Tamoxifen and Raloxifene [ STAR] trial) showed ral
oxifene is as effective as tamoxifen in reducing the risk of invasive breast
cancer with a lower side-effect proHle .

b. Raloxifene does not reduce the risk of noninvasive breast cancers.

## 3 . Oophorectomy

a. Removal of both ovaries can reduce the risk of breast cancer by approximately
50% in high-risk women.

4. Prophylactic mastectomy
a. Should be performed only after complete risk assessment and thorough
discussion with the patient
b. Should remove the entire breast using the same boundaries as therapeutic
mastectomy
c. Skin-sparing mastectomy may be used to facilitate immediate reconstruction.

D. Hereditary breast cancer
l . About 5% to 1 0% of breast cancers are attributed to inherited mutations , most
of which are caused by mutations of BRCAI and BRCA2 .

2. BRCAI and BRCA2 are tumor suppressor genes that have roles in DNA repair
and regulation of gene expression.

3. Mutations of both genes carry a lifetime risk of breast cancer of 40% to 85%
and are associated with an increased risk of ovarian cancer, with 30% to 45%
lifetime risk with BRCAI and 10% to 20% with BRCA2 mutation.

4. There is also an increased risk of male breast cancer in BRCA families , with
1% to 5% lifetime risk with BRCAI and 5% to 10% with BRCA2 mutation.

5. Other genetic breast cancer syndromes include Li-Fraumeni, Cowden, and
Lynch syndromes. Li-Fraumeni syndrome is associated with mutation in the
tumor suppressor gene p53.

E . Proliferative lesions
l . ALH
a. Composed of cells similar to those found in LCIS but less than half the
acini are filled or distorted
b. Associated with a fivefold relative risk of developing breast cancer

## 2. ADH

a. Marked proliferation and atypia of the epithelium
b. Found in 3% of benign breast biopsies
c. Associated with a 13% development of breast cancer (fourfold risk)
d. Diagnosed by the same criteria as D CIS but does not have all the characteris
tics necessary to diagnose intraductal cancer

## 3. LCIS

a. An incidental Hnding or core or excisional biopsy or marker
b. Patients have an 8 to 10 times risk, or about a 1% per year risk of developing
invasive carcinoma in the same or opposite breast.

c. Treatment may be observation, antihormonal (tamoxifen) , or bilateral
mastectomies, depending on patient preference.
```
